title: Synaptic Signals
subject: Why the future of AI looks a lot like the human brain
content:
The Bio-Inspired Shift

Hi Kelvin,

Welcome to this week's edition of Synaptic Signals. For decades, artificial intelligence has borrowed heavily from biology—but as our current models scale to massive, energy-hungry proportions, researchers are once again looking to the human brain for the next major breakthrough.

This week, we are exploring the intersection of computational modeling and biological brains, and how bio-inspired architecture is solving some of AI's biggest bottlenecks.

The Plasticity Problem

Most modern neural networks are static once trained. If you want them to learn something entirely new, you often have to retrain them from scratch, which requires massive computational resources.

Biological brains, however, rely on neuroplasticity—the ability to continuously rewire and adapt to new information without forgetting past knowledge. Integrating continuous, lifelong learning into AI frameworks is the next frontier, moving us away from rigid models and toward adaptable, agentic systems that learn on the fly.

Doing More with Less: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)

The human brain runs on about 20 watts of power (roughly enough to power a dim lightbulb), yet it outperforms megawatt-consuming supercomputers in complex, generalized tasks. How?

Unlike traditional Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) that process continuous values at every layer, biological neurons "fire" only when a specific threshold is reached. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) mimic this event-driven architecture, transmitting information via discrete spikes. This dramatically reduces energy consumption and latency, making them highly efficient for real-world, dynamic data processing.

Bio-Inspired Tools to Explore

If you are building computational models or experimenting with bio-inspired systems, here are a few resources worth diving into this week:

  • BindsNET: An open-source Python package built on top of PyTorch, designed specifically for simulating Spiking Neural Networks.

  • NEURON: A simulation environment for modeling individual neurons and networks of neurons, crucial for testing hypotheses in computational neuroscience.

  • Brian 2: A highly flexible, intuitive Python simulator tailored for spiking neural networks, allowing you to define models using standard mathematical notation.

Thought of the Week

"I think the brain is essentially a computer and consciousness is like a computer program. It will cease to run when the computer is turned off. Theoretically, it could be re-created on a neural network, but that would be very difficult, as it would require all one's memories."

— Stephen Hawking

Reply to this email and let us know what bio-inspired concepts you want us to break down next.

## The Plasticity Problem
Most modern neural networks are static once trained. If you want them to learn something entirely new, you often have to retrain them from scratch, which requires massive computational resources.

Biological brains, however, rely on **neuroplasticity**—the ability to continuously rewire and adapt to new information without forgetting past knowledge. Integrating continuous, lifelong learning into AI frameworks is the next frontier, moving us away from rigid models and toward adaptable, agentic systems that learn on the fly.

## Doing More with Less: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)
